# testdata

Sample inputs for poking at the CLI by hand. Not used by the Go test
suite (those tests generate their own fixtures).

## Files

| File                | Shape                                        | Use with            |
| ------------------- | -------------------------------------------- | ------------------- |
| `user.json`         | Single JSON object (~113 B)                  | `put --value-stdin` |
| `users.10.json`     | Pretty JSON array of 10 user objects         | `put --value-stdin` |
| `seed.ndjson`       | NDJSON, 8 PUTs + 1 DELETE (base64 k/v)       | `batch -`           |
| `seed.plain.ndjson` | Same payload as `seed.ndjson`, literal UTF-8 | `batch --plain -`   |
| `users.10.ndjson`   | 10 PUTs, literal UTF-8 user records          | `batch --plain -`   |
| `users.100.ndjson`  | 100 PUTs                                     | `batch --plain -`   |
| `users.1k.ndjson`   | 1 000 PUTs (~140 KB)                         | `batch --plain -`   |
| `users.10k.ndjson`  | 10 000 PUTs (~1.4 MB) — latency/throughput   | `batch --plain -`   |

The `users.*` files are regenerated by `./testdata/gen_users.sh` and
follow the shape `user:<id> -> {"id","name","email","age","role"}`.

## Quick start

In one shell:

```bash
./bin/little-db serve --data-dir ./data --log-level=debug
```

In another:

```bash
# Single large JSON value -> one key.
./bin/little-db put user:42 --value-stdin < testdata/user.json
./bin/little-db get user:42                       # echoes the JSON back

# Bulk load 8 PUTs + 1 DELETE atomically per line, single round trip.
./bin/little-db batch - < testdata/seed.ndjson

# Same payload, but human-readable: --plain takes key/value as literal
# UTF-8 strings (no base64). Convenient for hand-edited fixtures; cannot
# carry non-UTF-8 bytes.
./bin/little-db batch --plain - < testdata/seed.plain.ndjson

# Inspect.
./bin/little-db stats                                # KeyCount should be 8 (user:42 + 7 net from the batch)
./bin/little-db get user:1                           # -> {"name":"Alice",...}

# Scan every user:* key (';' is one byte past ':' in ASCII,
# so it is the exclusive upper bound for the 'user:' prefix).
./bin/little-db range --start 'user:' --end 'user;' --format raw
```

## Latency / throughput poking

```bash
# Bulk load increasing scales (single round-trip batches).
for n in 10 100 1k 10k; do
  echo "=== users.$n.ndjson ==="
  /usr/bin/time -p ./bin/little-db batch --plain - < testdata/users.$n.ndjson
done

# Single-key GET latency at scale.
./bin/little-db stats
./bin/little-db get user:9999

# Range scan throughput across the whole keyspace.
./bin/little-db range --start 'user:' --end 'user;' --format raw | wc -l
```

Regenerate the `users.*` fixtures (deterministic, no deps beyond awk):

```bash
./testdata/gen_users.sh
```

## Regenerating `seed.ndjson`

Keys and values in a batch line must be **base64-encoded** (the wire
protocol carries raw bytes; JSON cannot). To rebuild from any
`{k: v, ...}` JSON file with `jq`:

```bash
jq -r 'to_entries[]
       | {op:"put", key:(.key|@base64), value:(.value|tostring|@base64)}
       | @json' your.json > testdata/seed.ndjson
```
